title: Tapping for the Marriage Drift
subtitle: An EFT sequence for the slow distance that can grow in a long partnership
excerpt: An EFT tapping sequence for the drift that accumulates in a long relationship: the parallel lives, the conversations that stopped happening, the distance that built gradually and is now hard to name or close. Taps through the fear of what addressing it would mean, and toward a first small step.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. EFT framework developed by Gary Craig (mid-1990s).

practice.whenToUse: Use when you are aware of distance in a long relationship and the fear of addressing it is stopping you from doing so.

content.text: An EFT tapping sequence for the drift that happens in a long partnership: the parallel lives, the conversations that quietly stopped, the closeness that is still possible but feels far away. This sequence works on the fear that makes the distance easier to live with than the conversation that might close it.
New to tapping? Read
how tapping works
first.
The practice
Karate chop
Tap the karate chop point and say each statement once:
Even though we've drifted and I'm not sure how to come back from here, I deeply and completely accept myself.
Even though addressing it feels risky and living with it feels easier, I accept this is where I am right now.
Even though I don't know how to start the conversation and I'm afraid of what it might open, I am willing to be clear about where we are.
Tapping round
Tap each point seven times while saying the phrase:
Eyebrow: The distance between us that built slowly.
Side of Eye: The parallel lives we've been living.
Under Eye: What we used to have that I'm not sure we can get back.
Under Nose: The conversations we don't have anymore.
Chin: The fear of what we would find if we looked at it directly.
Collarbone: The effort it would take to close this gap.
Under Arm: Not knowing how to start without making it worse.
Top of Head: The drift that neither of us planned for.
Reframe
Eyebrow: Drift is reversible. It built slowly and it can close slowly.
Side of Eye: Parallel lives can converge. The pattern is not fixed.
Under Eye: What we had is still in us. It hasn't disappeared, it's been covered over.
Under Nose: One conversation doesn't have to solve everything. It just has to start something.
Chin: I can say what I want without it being an accusation.
Collarbone: I don't have to know all the steps. I only have to know one.
Under Arm: We've come back from hard things before.
Top of Head: I can take one step toward them without knowing where it leads.
Tap each round three times, then move to the reframe and tap that three times.
Where this practice comes from
Written for homemade.education. The EFT framework is Gary Craig's Emotional Freedom Technique, in use since the mid-1990s.
